patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET as previously done. The change is motivated by a desire to provide more accurate error logging and avoid misleading conclusions about system behavior.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be technically sound, but suggested that the author consider adding more documentation about the changes made.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns about the potential impact of logging all bio status codes and suggested that the author consider leaving things as-is for now, with a plan to revisit the issue in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().

This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
be very soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Responded to Christoph's concerns by arguing that logging all bio status codes would provide more visibility into system errors and help avoid misleading conclusions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 05:20:20PM +0100, Christoph Hellwig wrote:
> On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> > these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> > bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> > layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> > system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> > 
> > For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> > BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> > BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
> 442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
> on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
> even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
> I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
> to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
> be very soon.
> 

Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
already seeing? I have thousands of instances with swallowed
BLK_STS_MEDIUM errors that really confused me and messed up my data
while investigating a possible regression.

If you think this will confuse/upset users who aren't watching a giant
fleet with distributed logging and make them throw out good drives
prematurely or something, then I can just carry this patch internally.

If you are just worried about having to aim at a moving target, I am
happy to offer to be available to make sure btrfs does what you want
when you do find time to work on this. It's hard for me to judge since I
don't really know what is wrong with the error signalling as-is.

Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
rather than less.

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Suggested that the author consider adding BLK_STS_MEDIUM to the whitelist for now, and offered to revisit the issue in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 09:29:51AM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
> already seeing?

For example if you get a BLK_STS_AGAIN which is not actually an
error.

> Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
> what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
> rather than less.

Can you give me a week to give this thing a stab?  If not please just
add STS_MEDIUM to the whitelist for now.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Notified the list that the patch had been removed from for-next and would be revisited when the BLK_STS_* changes were made.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Mar 17, 2026 at 02:59:42PM +0100, Christoph Hellwig wrote:
> On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 09:29:51AM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
> > already seeing?
> 
> For example if you get a BLK_STS_AGAIN which is not actually an
> error.
> 
> > Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
> > what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
> > rather than less.
> 
> Can you give me a week to give this thing a stab?  If not please just
> add STS_MEDIUM to the whitelist for now.

Boris has some trouble sending mail, I'm replying on his behalf. The
patch has been removed from for-next and we'll wait for the BLK_STS_*
changes.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Indicated that he was still in the exploration phase and suggested logging extra errors, but also mentioned that only a few status codes were considered real I/O errors.
review_comments.raw_body:
The work week is almost over, and I'm still in the explore and
experimentation phase.  So please go ahead and log extra error,
although traditionally only BLK_STS_IOERR, BLK_STS_PROTECTION and
BLK_STS_MEDIUM are real I/O ero I/O error.  The rest is unexpected
here, and maybe just logging a message about that would make sense
here.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces an explicit lock/unlock pattern in the __offline_isolated_pages() function with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock when it goes out of scope. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of lock-related bugs. The patch targets the mm/page_alloc.c file and is part of a larger series of patches.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `free_pcppages_bulk()` function to use a zone lock guard instead of an explicit lock/unlock pattern. The zone lock guard automatically cleans up the lock when it goes out of scope, simplifying the code and reducing the risk of lock-related bugs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 6/8] mm: use zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 6/8] mm: use zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the explicit lock/unlock pattern in the put_page_back_buddy() function with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock when it goes out of scope. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of lock-related errors. The patch targets the mm/page_alloc.c file and is part of a larger series of patches.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 5/8] mm: use zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 5/8] mm: use zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the explicit lock/unlock pattern in take_page_off_buddy() with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock when it goes out of scope. This change allows for direct return from the loop and simplifies the code.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unset_migratetype_isolate()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 2/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unset_migratetype_isolate()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the explicit lock/unlock pattern in the unset_migratetype_isolate() function with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock when it goes out of scope. This simplifies the code and reduces the risk of lock-related bugs. The change is made to improve the maintainability and reliability of the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unreserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unreserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the explicit lock/unlock pattern in the unreserve_highatomic_pageblock() function with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock when it goes out of scope. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of lock-related bugs. The problem this patch solves is the complexity and potential for errors in manual lock management, while the approach taken is to use a higher-level abstraction provided by the Linux kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 4/8] mm: use zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/8] mm: use zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the set_migratetype_isolate() function in the Linux kernel's mm/page_isolation.c file to use a scoped lock guard instead of an explicit lock/unlock pattern. The change is made to improve code readability and avoid potential lockdep splats by keeping dump_page() outside the locked section. The patch also removes redundant checks and simplifies the logic within the function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the explicit lock/unlock pattern in reserve_highatomic_pageblock() with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock when exiting the function's scope.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/8] mm: use spinlock guards for zone lock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/8] mm: use spinlock guards for zone lock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series simplifies the control flow in mm functions by replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup using spinlock guards for zone lock.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about introducing new code before -rc1 and emphasized that adoption of guard() is a matter of preference/style choice.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 27 Mar 2026 16:14:40 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> This series uses spinlock guard for zone lock across several mm
> functions to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic
> scope-based cleanup.
> 
> This simplifies the control flow by removing 'flags' variables, goto
> labels, and redundant unlock calls.
> 
> Patches are ordered by decreasing value. The first six patches simplify
> the control flow by removing gotos, multiple unlock paths, or 'ret'
> variables. The last two are simpler lock/unlock pair conversions that
> only remove 'flags' and can be dropped if considered unnecessary churn.

Thanks, you've been busy.

I'm not wanting to move new, non-fix, non-speedup things into mm.git
until after -rc1 so there's your target.  But now is a good time to be
sending out material for people to look at.  Let's not have a gigantic
flood of new stuff the day after -rc1!

I think progress here is totally dependent on whether those who
regularly work on this code want guard() in there.  A
preference/familiarity/style choice, mainly.  At present the adoption
of guard() in mm/*.c is very small.
